explanations.1: The stratum basale is the deepest layer of the epidermis and connects to the dermis via the dermal papillae. Other layers like the stratum corneum are more superficial and do not interface with the dermis.
explanations.2: Langerhans cells are specialized dendritic cells that act as macrophages and are primarily found interspersed among the keratinocytes of the stratum spinosum. They are not typically found in the stratum corneum or granulosum.
explanations.3: The dermis is composed of two layers of connective tissue: the papillary layer (loose connective tissue) and the reticular layer (dense irregular connective tissue). Melanocytes and keratinocytes are primarily epidermal cells.
explanations.4: Collagen is a fibrous protein that provides structural strength and toughness to the skin. Elastin provides elasticity, while melanin provides color and UV protection.
explanations.5: The epidermis is avascular, meaning it contains no blood vessels; therefore, the hypodermis cannot provide vessels to it. The hypodermis does function in fat storage, temperature regulation, and protection.
explanations.6: The arrector pili are smooth muscles that contract in response to sympathetic signals, pulling hair follicles and causing hair to stand up, known as goose bumps. They are not glands and do not secrete sebum.
explanations.7: The hair matrix is a layer of mitotically active basal cells at the base of the hair bulb that produces the hair strand. The hair shaft and follicle are structures associated with the hair but are not the matrix itself.
explanations.8: Eccrine sweat glands are found throughout the body and produce a watery secretion for thermoregulation. Sebaceous glands produce sebum, which acts as a moisturizer.
explanations.9: Sebaceous glands are exocrine glands that are usually associated with hair follicles, where they secrete sebum to lubricate and waterproof the hair and skin. They are distinct from sweat glands.
explanations.10: The hyponychium is the area beneath the free edge of the nail, furthest from the nail root where growth originates. The eponychium (cuticle) and nail root are much closer to the growth center.
explanations.11: Exposure to UV radiation triggers the skin to synthesize vitamin D from a derivative of cholesterol. UV light actually destroys folate rather than producing it.
explanations.12: The skin does not synthesize folic acid; rather, UV radiation can degrade folate in the body. The stratum lucidum and desmosomes provide physical protection, while Merkel cells provide sensory protection.
explanations.13: The entire epidermis (including the stratum corneum, granulosum, and basale) is avascular. To bleed, a cut must reach the papillary dermis, which contains the most superficial blood vessels.
explanations.14: The stratum corneum is the outermost layer consisting of dead, keratinized cells that form a waterproof barrier to prevent dehydration. Deeper layers like the stratum basale are not exposed to the air.
explanations.15: Langerhans cells are dendritic cells in the epidermis that function as immune cells by engulfing and digesting bacteria. Keratinocytes provide structure, while melanocytes provide pigment.
explanations.16: Most skin cancers are caused by DNA damage from UV radiation; therefore, limiting sun exposure directly reduces risk. Skin cancers are not caused by hygiene and can be life-threatening if they metastasize.
explanations.17: The presence of blisters indicates a second-degree burn, which signifies damage to both the epidermis and the underlying dermis. First-degree burns only affect the epidermis and do not blister.
explanations.18: After a clot forms, the next stage of healing involves fibroblasts mobilizing to produce connective tissue (collagen) to repair the site. Melanin and Pacinian corpuscles are not involved in the immediate repair of tissue.
explanations.19: Squamous cell carcinoma specifically affects the keratinocytes of the stratum spinosum. Basal cell carcinoma affects the stratum basale, and melanoma affects melanocytes.
